aliases: Deep Fried Special Marinated Pork Chop

english: Deep Fried Special Marinated Pork Chop

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pork chop food product entry; no specific deep-fried marinated pork chop entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific deep-fried marinated pork chop code. A0CKP (Pig meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226803007 covers pork chop as a food item; no specific deep-fried marinated variant code exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately one pork chop piece weighing around 150 g, as commonly served in Chinese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, loin, blade (chops), bone-in, cooked, pan-fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pan-fried pork loin chop (NDB No. 10062), adjusted upward for fat and energy to reflect deep-frying in oil and a spiced marinade coating (soy sauce, garlic, five-spice, egg, cornstarch). Energy, fat, and sodium values have been modestly increased relative to the pan-fried baseline to account for oil absorption during deep-frying (estimated 5–8 g additional fat per 100 g) and sodium contribution from soy-sauce marinade. This is an estimated adaptation; values should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis or HPB Singapore Foodo entry if one becomes available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried marinated pork chop. GI estimated at 45 based on analogy with battered and fried meat products with a starch coating (cornstarch-battered fried chicken and pork analogues from University of Sydney GI Database). The carbohydrate content is low (6.5 g per 100 g), primarily from the cornstarch-and-marinade coating. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (45 × 9.75 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.4 GL units, which is low. GI category: low (GI < 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-sauce marinade (~520 mg sodium per 100 g; ~780 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this dish due to its high sodium content from the soy-sauce-based marinade; a single 150 g serving provides approximately 780 mg sodium, representing over one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from cornstarch coating and high fat content from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in moderation; while the glycemic load per serving is low (approximately 4.4 GL units), the high total fat content from deep-frying (approximately 27.8 g fat per 150 g serving) contributes to excess caloric intake and may impair insulin sensitivity over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium from deep-frying and soy-sauce marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake of this dish as deep-frying significantly increases total fat, saturated fat (approximately 7.2 g per serving), and cholesterol (approximately 123 mg per serving), while the soy-sauce marinade contributes a high sodium load, all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-Fried Marinated Pork Chop (炸排骨, Zhá Páigǔ) is a popular Chinese dish in which bone-in or boneless pork chop cuts are marinated in a flavourful mixture of soy sauce, garlic, five-spice powder, and sometimes rice wine, then coated in a light cornstarch-and-egg batter before being deep-fried in hot oil until golden, crispy, and cooked through. The result is a dish with a crunchy exterior and juicy, tender interior that carries the aromatic complexity of the spice marinade. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served in Chinese restaurants and hawker stalls, and is a signature item at establishments such as Din Tai Fung. Nutritionally, a 150 g serving provides approximately 428 kcal, 33.8 g protein, 27.8 g fat, and 9.8 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~780 mg per serving) due to the soy-sauce marinade, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The deep-frying process substantially increases total fat and saturated fat relative to pan-fried or grilled pork preparations. The glycemic load per serving is low (approximately 4.4 GL units) because the carbohydrate content is modest and primarily from the cornstarch coating. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher diets. It is a good source of protein, B vitamins (particularly B1, B3, B6), zinc, selenium, and phosphorus.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for deep-fried marinated pork chop was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN at the time of processing. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pan-fried pork loin chop data with estimated adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption and soy-sauce sodium contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for comparable battered fried meat products. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA base data is high quality and the adaptation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (pan-fried pork loin chop, NDB No. 10062) with estimated upward adjustments for deep-frying fat absorption (~5–8 g additional fat per 100 g) and soy-sauce sodium contribution. A direct laboratory analysis or HPB Singapore Foodo entry, if available, should supersede these values. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked pork loin; these are not -1 sentinel values but estimated numeric values from the adapted source. 3. GI is estimated at 45 (low category) by analogy with cornstarch-battered fried meat products from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was located. 4. The dish is served at Din Tai Fung Marina Bay Link Mall in Singapore at SGD 14.26 per portion (source: field survey data, preserved from input record for commercial context only; not used as a nutritional source). 5. Halal status is false as pork is not halal. 6. All numeric fields are populated with estimated values from the adapted USDA source; no -1 sentinel values are used in this record as reasonable estimates were available for all fields.
